signal_comments.author: @Drona666
signal_comments.text:
Instead of keeping fixed roles (Generator vs Evaluator), the system alternates roles after each iteration. This forces both agents to:

Critically analyze outputs
Apply their own feedback
Reduce bias and stagnation in improvements
Step-by-Step Process
Step 1: Initialize Roles
Agent A → Generator
Agent B → Evaluator
Step 2: Define Objective

Provide a clear goal and quality threshold:

Task: Design a professional logo
Target: Minimum 9.5/10 quality score
Step 3: First Generation
Agent A creates an initial logo design based on the objective.
Step 4: Evaluation
Agent B evaluates the output:
Assigns a score (e.g., 6.5/10)
Provides specific, actionable feedback, such as:
Improve typography consistency
Refine color palette
Enhance brand relevance
Increase visual balance
Step 5: Role Swap
Agent B becomes the Generator
Agent A becomes the Evaluator
Step 6: Feedback-Driven Regeneration
The new Generator (Agent B) creates an improved version using:
Its own prior feedback
The original goal and constraints
Step 7: Re-Evaluation
The new Evaluator (Agent A):
Scores the updated output
Provides further refinement suggestions
Step 8: Iterate with Continuous Role Swapping

Repeat:

Swap roles after every cycle
Each iteration must:
Improve on prior feedback
Move closer to the target score
Step 9: Convergence Condition

Stop when:

Output reaches ≥ 9.5/10, or
Improvements become marginal (diminishing returns)
Why This Works
Prevents evaluator stagnation: Evaluator must “prove” its critique by generating better output.
Improves feedback quality: Feedback becomes more practical and implementable.
Reduces bias loops: No single agent dominates direction.
Encourages accountability: Each agent is responsible for both critique and execution.
Optional Enhancements
Add a memory log of past scores and changes
Use multi-criteria scoring (e.g., creativity, clarity, brand fit)
Introduce a third “judge” agent for final validation
Apply temperature variation: higher for generation, lower for evaluation

signal_comments.text:
I'm actually setting up Clawd right now, and so far it's not that diffrent from Codex or stuff we have had for a while, the biggest thing is that you can have your LLM use the browser from your local machine instead of inside the cloud, and that actually opens up a good use case for the local llm's like Qwent TTS, now I can use a cheap API licens like gemini flash to be the director of an Audio book factory at the fraction of the cost, or even free (electricity cost and hardware cost excluded) 
Clawd is not some bot that have a constant awerness and starts doing things on its own, it does one task and follows it through and then wait's for next input, wheter it comes from a chat or cron job, it's still needs something to trigger the next task.

signal_comments.author: @bryanslab25
signal_comments.text:
Look at what people are already paying other humans to do.

3 categories for AI startup: 
- assist humans in performing their jobs better and faster
- replace and automate an entire job
- accomplish previously too expensive or time consuming task like analyzing millions of legal docs instantly.

Reliable AI products > Flashy demos
Understand the job deeply, break down the process into specific steps, translate to code, evaluate

Great Product > Marketing
But u still need to do marketing.
Your not selling a $20/month tool, your augmenting or replacing human labor. Try price per seat
AI is new and scary, so build trust with pilots and comparisons with existing human workflow to prove effectiveness
Customer signed != job done
Do onboarding, training, support ensure customer uses the product fully.
The entire experience is the product, not just software.

signal_comments.author: @PrathameshAdsul-z8m
signal_comments.text:
*The purpose of the talking stage (and why it shouldn’t be skipped)*

The talking stage isn’t a waste of time. It’s the foundation.

It exists so you can assess compatibility, not just attraction. It’s where you observe patterns instead of falling in love with potential. *Anyone can impress you for a few days, but consistency shows over time.*

The talking stage  helps clarify intentions early. Are you both looking for the same thing or just enjoying attention? 
Talking prevents silent assumptions that later turn into disappointment.

It’s also where communication styles are revealed. 
How someone handles disagreements, boundaries, silence, and emotions will show long before a title does.

Most importantly, the talking stage builds emotional safety. Trust grows through conversation, honesty, and time, not through rushing labels.

signal_comments.author: @DollarswithDiana
signal_comments.text:
Thanks for this video, I'm figuring out exactly what I want my AI agent to do for me! Video Notes Below:
🤖Q: What exactly is an AI agent and how does it differ from simple automation? A: AI agents function as digital employees that can reason, plan, and take actions using LLMs for multi-step reasoning, memory for context and knowledge, and tools for integrations and actions, unlike traditional automation that follows fixed if-then rules.

🎯Q: What types of tasks should you automate first when building AI agents? A: Start with low precision, time-intensive, structured data tasks that have clear success metrics where 90% accuracy is acceptable, such as research, compilation, background tasks, which can be automated in hours or minutes instead of much longer manual work.

📋Q: What's the critical step before automating any process with AI agents? A: Documenting and optimizing processes before automation reveals inefficiencies and redundancies that can be addressed even without automation, using AI to analyze and spot issues that may have been missed in existing workflows.

🛡Q: How should you approach building AI agents to avoid common failures? A: Build the simplest working version first, then gradually add complexity while keeping humans in the loop for quality assurance, implementing guardrails to prevent hallucinations and bad decisions, and tracking metrics for efficiency, quality, and business impact.

⚙Q: What's the practical difference between Zapier and N8N for building AI agents? A: Zapier serves as the easy autopilot for simple tasks with built-in integrations, while N8N functions as the advanced cockpit for deep customization, complex logic requiring reasoning and adaptability, and understanding what's happening under the hood.

🧠Q: What powers an AI agent's decision-making capabilities? A: The AI agent's brain is an LLM model like OpenAI's GPT-5 connected via API key, used for tasks like research, writing, and decision-making based on the agent's prompt and system instructions.

📚Q: What free resource provides a complete framework for implementing AI agents? A: The 2026 AI Agents Guide by Futurepedia and HubSpot provides frameworks, worksheets, implementation details, and a roadmap for assessing automation opportunities, integrating agents, and tracking ROI.

🔍Q: How can research tools be integrated into AI agents for information gathering? A: Perplexity can be integrated into AI agents to gather information from various sources, while Google Docs can create and update documents based on the agent's findings and formatting instructions.

results.video_title: Multi Agent Systems Explained: How AI Agents & LLMs Work Together

signal_comments.author: @iosascha1
signal_comments.text: Good overview of the architectural patterns. The key gap in most multi-agent discussions is the cost dimension — these systems don't just add complexity, they multiply inference costs. In a hierarchical pattern, the orchestrator agent doesn't need the same model as the specialist workers. A cheaper model can handle coordination, routing, and status checks while only the agents doing complex reasoning or content generation need the expensive tier. In a decentralized pattern, you can even have agents dynamically negotiate which model tier to use based on task complexity — essentially model cascading at the agent communication layer. Without this, production multi-agent systems hit cost walls very quickly, especially with re-planning loops that can spiral token usage.

results.video_title: From Idea to $650M Exit: Lessons in Building AI Startups

signal_comments.author: @bryanslab25
signal_comments.text:
Look at what people are already paying other humans to do.

3 categories for AI startup: 
- assist humans in performing their jobs better and faster
- replace and automate an entire job
- accomplish previously too expensive or time consuming task like analyzing millions of legal docs instantly.

Reliable AI products > Flashy demos
Understand the job deeply, break down the process into specific steps, translate to code, evaluate

Great Product > Marketing
But u still need to do marketing.
Your not selling a $20/month tool, your augmenting or replacing human labor. Try price per seat
AI is new and scary, so build trust with pilots and comparisons with existing human workflow to prove effectiveness
Customer signed != job done
Do onboarding, training, support ensure customer uses the product fully.
The entire experience is the product, not just software.

signal_comments.author: @PrathameshAdsul-z8m
signal_comments.text:
*The purpose of the talking stage (and why it shouldn’t be skipped)*

The talking stage isn’t a waste of time. It’s the foundation.

It exists so you can assess compatibility, not just attraction. It’s where you observe patterns instead of falling in love with potential. *Anyone can impress you for a few days, but consistency shows over time.*

The talking stage  helps clarify intentions early. Are you both looking for the same thing or just enjoying attention? 
Talking prevents silent assumptions that later turn into disappointment.

It’s also where communication styles are revealed. 
How someone handles disagreements, boundaries, silence, and emotions will show long before a title does.

Most importantly, the talking stage builds emotional safety. Trust grows through conversation, honesty, and time, not through rushing labels.
